name: Mock Test - 03
instruction: This question paper contains three sections - A, B and C. Each part is compulsory. Section - A has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20. Section - B has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20 Section - C has 10 MCQs, attempt any 8 out of 10. There is no negative marking. All questions carry equal marks.

questions.question_text: The function f : A {tex}\to{/tex} B defined by f(x) = -x 2 + 6x - 8 is a bijection, if
questions.question_html: The function f : A {tex}\to{/tex} B defined by f(x) = -x2 + 6x - 8 is a bijection, if
questions.explanation: Given that f : A {tex}\to{/tex} B defined by f(x) = -x 2 + 6x - 8 is a bijection. f(x) = -x 2 + 6x - 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = -(x 2 - 6x + 8) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = - (x 2 - 6x + 8 + 1 - 1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = - (x 2 - 6x + 9 - 1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = - [(x - 3) 2 - 1] Hence, {tex}x \in(-\infty, 3] \text { and } f(x) \in(-\infty, 1]{/tex}

questions.question_text: In a LPP, the linear inequalities or restrictions on the variables are called
questions.question_html: In a LPP, the linear inequalities or restrictions on the variables are called
questions.explanation: In a LPP, the linear inequalities or restrictions on the variables are called Linear constraints.

questions.question_text: If f(x) = (cos x + i sin x) (cos 2x + i sin 2x) (cos 3x + i sin 3x) ... (cos nx + i sin nx) and f(1) = 1, then f"(1) is equal to
questions.question_html: If f(x) = (cos x + i sin x) (cos 2x + i sin 2x) (cos 3x + i sin 3x) ... (cos nx + i sin nx) and f(1) = 1, then f"(1) is equal to
questions.explanation: f(x) = (cosx + i sinx) (cos 2x + i sin 2x) (cos 3x + i sin 3x) ..., (cos nx + i sin nx) {tex}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{ix}} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i} 2 \mathrm{x}} \ldots \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{inx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i} (1+2+3 \ldots+\mathrm{n})x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{i x \frac{n(n+1)}{2}}{/tex} As, f(1) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow e^{i \frac{n(n+1)}{2}}=1{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\operatorname{in}(n+1)}{2} e^{i x \frac{n(n+1)}{2}}{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{i^{2} n^{2}(n+1)^{2}}{4} e^{i x \frac{n(n+1)}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{n^{2}(n+1)^{2}}{4} e^{i x \frac{n(n+1)}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore ~f^{\prime \prime}(1)=-\frac{n^{2}(n+1)^{2}}{4} e^{\frac{n(n+1)}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{n^{2}(n+1)^{2}}{4} f(1)=-\frac{n^{2}(n+1)^{2}}{4}{/tex}

questions.question_html: If area of triangle is 35 sq units with vertices (2, -6), (5, 4) and (k, 4). Then k is

questions.question_text: In an LPP if the objective function Z = ax + by has the same maximum value on two corner points of the feasible region, then every point on the line segment joining these two points give the same ________ value.
questions.question_html: In an LPP if the objective function Z = ax + by has the same maximum value on two corner points of the feasible region, then every point on the line segment joining these two points give the same ________ value.
questions.explanation: In an LPP if the objective function Z = ax + by has the same maximum value on two corner points of the feasible region, then every point on the line segment joining these two points give the same maximum value . If the problem has multiple optimal solutions at the corner points, then both the points will have the same (maximum or minimum)value.

questions.question_text: If y = tan -1 {tex}\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.question_html: If y = tan-1 {tex}\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.explanation: Given that {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}\right){/tex} Using {tex}\cos x=\cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}-\sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\sin x=2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta=1{/tex} Therefore, {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}-\sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}{\cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}+\sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}+2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)}{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\tan ^{-1} \frac{\cos \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}-\sin \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}}{\cos \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}+\sin \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}}{/tex} Dividing by cos {tex}\frac {x} {2}{/tex} in numerator and denominator, we get {tex}y=\tan ^{-1} \frac{1-\tan \frac{x}{2}}{1+\tan \frac{x}{2}}{/tex} Using {tex}\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right)=\frac{1-\tan x}{1+\tan x}{/tex} , we obtain {tex}y=\tan ^{-1} \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, we {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: For any 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix, If A(adj A) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 10 & 0 \\ 0 & 10 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then |A| is equal to
questions.question_html: For any 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix, If A(adj A) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 10 & 0 \\ 0 & 10 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then |A| is equal to
questions.explanation: We know that A {tex}\times{/tex} adjA = |A| I nxn , where I is the unit matrix of order nxn.--------------------[1] {tex}A(\operatorname{adj} A)=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 10 & 0 \\ 0 & 10 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Using the above property of matrices (1), we get {tex}A(\operatorname{adj} A)=10\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A(adj A) = (10) I 2x2 |A| I 2x2 = 10 I 2x2 |A| = 10

questions.question_text: Minimize Z = x + 2y subject to 2x + y ≥ 3, x + 2y ≥ 6, x, y ≥ 0.
questions.question_html: Minimize Z = x + 2y subject to 2x + y ≥ 3, x + 2y ≥ 6, x, y ≥ 0.

options.text: Minimum Z = 9 at all the points (6, 0) and (0, 3)
options.html: Minimum Z = 9 at all the points (6, 0) and (0, 3)

options.text: Minimum Z = 8 at all the points on the line segment joining the points (6, 0) and (0, 3)
options.html: Minimum Z = 8 at all the points on the line segment joining the points (6, 0) and (0, 3)

options.text: Minimum Z = 7 at all the points on the line segment joining the points (6, 0) and (0, 3)
options.html: Minimum Z = 7 at all the points on the line segment joining the points (6, 0) and (0, 3)

options.text: Minimum Z = 6 at all the points on the line segment joining the points (6, 0) and (0, 3)
options.html: Minimum Z = 6 at all the points on the line segment joining the points (6, 0) and (0, 3)

questions.correct_option_text: Minimum Z = 6 at all the points on the line segment joining the points (6, 0) and (0, 3)
questions.explanation: Objective function is Z = x + 2 y ……………………(1). The given constraints are : 2x + y ≥ 3, x + 2y ≥ 6, x, y ≥ 0 . Corner points Z = x + 2y A(0 ,3 ) 6…………………..(Minimum) B(6,0) 6………………………(Minimum) Here , Z = 18 is minimum at ( 0, 3 ) and ( 6 , 0 ) . Minimum Z = 6 at all the points on the line segment joining the points (6, 0) and (0, 3).

questions.question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} i & 0 \\ 0 & i \end{array}\right]{/tex} , n {tex}\in{/tex} N, then A 4n equals
questions.question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} i & 0 \\ 0 & i \end{array}\right]{/tex}, n {tex}\in{/tex} N, then A4n equals
questions.explanation: A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & i \\ i & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A 4n = A 4 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & \mathrm{i} \\ \mathrm{i} & 0 \end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & \mathrm{i} \\ \mathrm{i} & 0 \end{array}\right] \times{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ i & 0 \end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & i \\ i & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {n = 1, so the exponent comes out to be 4 and if n = 2, which will turn the exponent to 8, and the same cycle will repeat.} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} \mathrm{i}^{4} & 0 \\ 0 & \mathrm{i}^{4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} (-1)^{2} & 0 \\ 0 & (-1)^{2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

questions.question_text: If {tex}y=\log \sqrt{\tan x}{/tex} , then the value of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} is given by
questions.question_html: If {tex}y=\log \sqrt{\tan x}{/tex}, then the value of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} is given by
questions.explanation: {tex}y=\log \sqrt{\tan x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\tan x}} \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\tan x}} \sec ^{2} x{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sec ^{2} x}{2 \tan x}{/tex} {tex}\left|\frac{d y}{d x}\right|_{x=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{\sec ^{2} \frac{\pi}{4}}{2 {\tan \frac{\pi}{4}}}=\frac{2}{2 \times 1}=1{/tex}

questions.question_text: Minimize Z = 3x + 5y such that x + 3y ≥ 3, x + y ≥ 2, x, y ≥ 0.
questions.question_html: Minimize Z = 3x + 5y such that x + 3y ≥ 3, x + y ≥ 2, x, y ≥ 0.

options.html: Minimum Z = 9 at {tex}\left( {\frac{3}{2},\;\frac{1}{2}} \right){/tex} 110 V 60 Hz

questions.explanation: Objective function is Z = 3x + 5 y ……………………(1). The given constraints are : x + 3y ≥ 3, x + y ≥ 2, x, y ≥ 0 . The corner points obtained by drawing the lines x+3y=3 and x+y=2 are (0,0), (3,0),(0,2) and (3/2,1/2) Corner points Z = 3x + 5 y A(3 , 0 ) 9 B ( 0 ,2 ) 10 C( 3/2 , 1/2 ) 7………………..( Min. ) Here Z = 7 is minimum at ( {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} )

questions.question_text: Let f(x) {tex}=\left\{\begin{aligned} \frac{x^{2}-2 x-3}{x+1^{2}}, & \text { when } x \neq-1 \\ k, & \text { when } x=-1 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} If f(x) is continuous at x = -1 then k =
questions.question_html: Let f(x) {tex}=\left\{\begin{aligned} \frac{x^{2}-2 x-3}{x+1^{2}}, & \text { when } x \neq-1 \\ k, & \text { when } x=-1 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} If f(x) is continuous at x = -1 then k =
questions.explanation: We have, {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\frac{x^{2}-2 x-3}{x+1}{/tex} is continuous at x = 0. {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1} \frac{(x+1)(x-3)}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1} x-3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = -4 {tex}\therefore{/tex} k = -4

questions.question_text: If {tex}f(x)=x^{2} \sin \frac{1}{x}{/tex} where {tex}x \neq 0{/tex} then the value of the function f at x = 0, so that the function is continuous at x = 0, is
questions.question_html: If {tex}f(x)=x^{2} \sin \frac{1}{x}{/tex}where {tex}x \neq 0{/tex} then the value of the function f at x = 0, so that the function is continuous at x = 0, is
questions.explanation: We have, {tex} f(x)=x^{2} \sin \left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} , where x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 Since the function is continuous at x = 0, we have f(0) = {tex}\underset{x\rightarrow0}{lim}{/tex} {tex}x^{2} \sin \left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} ...(i) Now, {tex}-1 \le sin \frac 1x \le 1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-x^2 \le x^2sin \frac 1x \le x^2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\underset{x\rightarrow0}{lim}(-x^2) \le \underset{x\rightarrow0}{lim}(x^2sin \frac 1x) \le \underset{x\rightarrow0}{lim}(x^2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow0 \le \underset{x\rightarrow0}{lim}(x^2sin \frac 1x) \le 0{/tex} Therefore by squeeze principle, we have f(0) = {tex} \underset{x\rightarrow0}{lim}~(x^2sin \frac 1x) = 0{/tex} Hence, value of the function f at x=0 so that it is continuous at x=0 is 0.

questions.question_text: The derivative of an even function is
questions.question_html: The derivative of an even function is
questions.explanation: The derivative of an even function is an odd function.

questions.question_text: The equation of the normal to the curve y = x(2 - x) at the point (2, 0) is
questions.question_html: The equation of the normal to the curve y = x(2 - x) at the point (2, 0) is
questions.explanation: y = x(2 - x) y = 2x - x 2 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=2-2 x{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(2,0)}=-2{/tex} Slope of normal {tex}=-\frac{1}{\frac{d y}{d x}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Equation of normal, {tex}y-0=\frac{1}{2}(x-2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 2y = 2

questions.question_text: tan [2 tan -1 {tex}\frac{1}{5}-\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} ] = ?
questions.question_html: tan [2 tan-1 {tex}\frac{1}{5}-\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}] = ?
questions.explanation: The given equation is of tan [2 tan -1 {tex}\frac{1}{5}-\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} ] Let tan(2 tan -1 {tex}\frac{1}{5}-\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} ) = tan(tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{2\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)}{1-\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{2}}\right)-\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} ) ( {tex}\because{/tex} 2 tan -1 x = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{2 x}{1-x^{2}}\right){/tex} ) = tan(tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{\frac{2}{5}}{1-\frac{1}{25}}\right)-\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} ) = tan(tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{5}{12}\right)-\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} ) = tan(tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{5}{12}\right)-\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} ) = tan(tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{5}{12}\right){/tex} - tan -1 (1)) ( {tex}\because{/tex} tan {tex}(\frac{\pi}{4}){/tex} = 1) = tan(tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{\frac{5}{12}-1}{1+\frac{5}{12}}\right){/tex} ) (tan -1 x - tan -1 y = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{x-y}{1+x y}\right){/tex} = tan (tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{-7}{17}\right){/tex} ) tan(2 tan ​​​​​​​-1 {tex}\left.\frac{1}{5}-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{-7}{17}{/tex}

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\;\frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\;\frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\underset{x\to 0}{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{1-\cos 4x}{{{x}^{2}}}{/tex} {tex} =\underset{x\to 0}{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{4\sin 4x}{2x}{/tex} {tex} =\underset{x\to 0}{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{16\cos 4x}{2}=8 {/tex} ( using L’Hospital Rule)

questions.question_text: The maximum value of f(x) = {tex}\frac{x}{4+x+x^{2}}{/tex} on [-1, 1] is
questions.question_html: The maximum value of f(x) = {tex}\frac{x}{4+x+x^{2}}{/tex} on [-1, 1] is
questions.explanation: f(x) = {tex}\frac{x}{4+x+x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = {tex}\frac{4-x^{2}}{\left(4+x+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} For a local maxima or minima, f'(x) = 0 {tex}\frac{4-x^{2}}{\left(4+x+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex} 2 {tex}\in{/tex} [-1, 1] f(1) = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} > 0 f(-1) = {tex}\frac{-1}{4}{/tex} < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} is the maximum value.

questions.question_text: The function {tex}f(x) = \frac{x}{{1 + \left| X \right|}}{/tex} is
questions.question_html: The function {tex}f(x) = \frac{x}{{1 + \left| X \right|}}{/tex} is
questions.explanation: {tex}f(x) = \frac{x}{{1 + \left| x \right|}}{/tex} The domain of f = R. here, {tex}f'(x) = \frac{1}{{{{(1 + \left| x \right|)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} f ’(x) > 0 for all x belongs to R. Hence, f is strictly increasing function on R.

questions.question_text: If y = x 2 sin {tex}\frac {1} {x}{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.question_html: If y = x2 sin {tex}\frac {1} {x}{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.explanation: Given that y = x 2 sin {tex}\frac {1} {x}{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=x^{2} \cos \frac{1}{x} \times-\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}+2 x \sin \frac{1}{x}=2 x \sin \frac{1}{x}-\cos \frac{1}{x}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If y = sin -1 (3x - 4x 3 ) then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.question_html: If y = sin-1 (3x - 4x3) then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.explanation: Given that y = sin -1 (3x - 4x 3 ) Let x = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\sin ^{-1} x{/tex} Then, {tex}y=\sin ^{-1}\left(3 \sin \theta-4 \sin ^{3} \theta\right){/tex} Using {tex}\sin 3 \theta=3 \sin \theta-4 \sin ^{3} \theta{/tex} , we get {tex}y=\sin ^{-1}(\sin 3 \theta)=3 \theta=3 \sin ^{-1} x{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Let {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{x-4}{|x-4|}+a, & x<4 \\ a+b, & x=4 \\ \frac{x-4}{|x-4|}+b, & x>4 \end{array}\right.{/tex} Then, f(x) is continuous at x = 4 when
questions.question_html: Let {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{x-4}{|x-4|}+a, & x<4 \\ a+b, & x=4 \\ \frac{x-4}{|x-4|}+b, & x>4 \end{array}\right.{/tex} Then, f(x) is continuous at x = 4 when
questions.explanation: {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 4^-} f(x)=a+b{/tex} {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 4} \frac{x-4}{|x-4|}+a=a+b{/tex} - 1 + a = a + b - 1 = b Also, {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 4^+} f(x)+b=a+b{/tex} {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 4} \frac{x-4}{|x-4|}+b=a+b{/tex} 1 + b = a + b a = 1

questions.question_text: The function f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R defined by f (x) = 6 x + 6 |x| is
questions.question_html: The function f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R defined by f (x) = 6x + 6|x| is
questions.explanation: Given that function f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R defined by f(x) = 6 x + 6 |x| Let f(x) = f(y) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6 x + 6 |x| = 6 y + 6 |y| Only when x = y So, f is one-one. Now for y = f(x) y can never be negative which means for no x {tex}\in{/tex} R y is negative. So, f is not onto but into.

questions.question_text: Differentiate {tex}2 {e}^{{x}^4} \log x{/tex} w.r.t x .
questions.question_html: Differentiate {tex}2 {e}^{{x}^4} \log x{/tex} w.r.t x .
questions.explanation: Consider {tex}y=2 e^{x^4} \log x{/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(2 {e}^{{x}^4} \log {x}\right){/tex} Differentiating w.r.t {tex}x{/tex} by using chain rule, we get {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=2\left(\log x \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x^4}\right)+e^{x^4} \frac{d}{d x}(\log x)\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=2\left(\log x \cdot e^{x^4} \frac{d}{d x} x^4+e^{x^4} \cdot \frac{1}{x}\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=2\left(\log x \cdot e^{x^4} \cdot 4 x^3+e^{x^4} \cdot \frac{1}{x}\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=2 e^{x^4}\left(4 x^3 \log x+\frac{1}{x}\right) {/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\left(2 e^{x^4}\left(4 x^4 \log x+1\right)\right)}{x} {/tex}

questions.question_text: {tex}\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{11}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{2}{11}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{11}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{2}{11}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{11}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{2}{11}{/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{\frac{1}{11}+\frac{2}{11}}{1-\frac{2}{11} \times \frac{1}{11}}\right){/tex} = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{\frac{3}{11}}{1-\frac{2}{121}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{33}{119}\right){/tex}

questions.question_text: The value of det. {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&0&0&0 \\ 2&b&0&0 \\ 4&6&c&0 \\ 6&8&{10}&d \end{array}} \right]{/tex} is
questions.question_html: The value of det.{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&0&0&0 \\ 2&b&0&0 \\ 4&6&c&0 \\ 6&8&{10}&d \end{array}} \right]{/tex} is
questions.explanation: The determinant of a lower triangular or an upper triangular matrix is equal to the product of the diagonal elements.

questions.question_text: Maximize Z = 50x + 60y , subject to constraints x +2 y ≤ 50 , x +y ≥ 30, x, y ≥ 0.
questions.question_html: Maximize Z = 50x + 60y , subject to constraints x +2 y ≤ 50 , x +y ≥ 30, x, y ≥ 0.
questions.explanation: Here , Maximize Z = 50x+60y , subject to constraints x +2 y ≤ 50 , x + y ≥ 30, x, y ≥ 0. Corner points Z = 50x +60 y P( 50 ,0 ) 2500 Q(0 , 30) 1800 R( 10, 20 ) 1700 Hence, the maximum value is 2500

questions.question_text: If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ a & b & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then aI + bA + 2A 2 equals.
questions.question_html: If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ a & b & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then aI + bA + 2A2 equals.
questions.explanation: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ a & b & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}A^{2}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1+a & b & 3 \\ a & b & 2 \\ 3 a & 2 b & a+b+4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a I+b A+2 A^{2}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 a+2+b & 2 b & 6+b \\ 2 a & a+2 b & b+4 \\ a b 6 a & 6 b+b^{2} & 3 a+4 b+8 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

questions.question_text: The rate of change of the area of a circle with respect to its radius r at r = 6 cm is
questions.question_html: The rate of change of the area of a circle with respect to its radius r at r = 6 cm is
questions.explanation: We know that area of a circle (A) is A = {tex}\pi r^2{/tex} Then, Rate of change of the area with respect to its radius is given by {tex}\frac{d}{dr} A = \frac{d}{dr}\pi {r}^{2}=2 \pi \mathrm{r}{/tex} When r = 6 cm Then {tex}\frac{d }{d r}A=2 \pi(6)=12 \pi{/tex} Therefore, the area of the circle is changing at the rate of 12 {tex}\pi{/tex} cm 2 /s when its radius is 6 cm

questions.question_text: If both f and g are defined in a nhd of 0; f(0) = 0 = g(0) and f‘(0) = 8 = g’(0), then {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\;\frac{{f(x)}}{{g(x)}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If both f and g are defined in a nhd of 0; f(0) = 0 = g(0) and f‘(0) = 8 = g’(0), then {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\;\frac{{f(x)}}{{g(x)}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{f(x)}}{{g(x)}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{f'(x)}}{{g'(x)}} = \frac{{f'(0)}}{{g'(0)}} = 1{/tex} ( by using L’Hospital Rule )

questions.question_text: Let f (x) = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} Then, range (f) = ?
questions.question_html: Let f (x) = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} Then, range (f) = ?
questions.explanation: f(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} Range of f(x) can be found out by putting f(x) = y y = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - yx 2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} yx 2 = y - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = {tex}\frac{y-1}{y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{y-1}{y}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{y-1}{y}} \geq 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y {tex}\ge{/tex} 1 Hence, Range of the f(x) = [1, {tex}\infty{/tex} )

questions.question_text: Let {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin –1 (sin (– 600°)), then value of {tex}\theta{/tex} is
questions.question_html: Let {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin–1 (sin (– 600°)), then value of {tex}\theta{/tex} is
questions.explanation: sin -1 sin {tex}\left(-600 \times \frac{\pi}{180}\right)=\sin ^{-1} \sin \left(\frac{-10 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} = sin -1 {tex}\left[-\sin \left(4 \pi-\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right)\right]=\sin ^{-1}\left(\sin \frac{2 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} = sin -1 {tex}\left(\sin \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\sin \frac{\pi}{3}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Feasible region (shaded) for a LPP is shown in the Figure. Minimum of Z = 4x + 3y occurs at the point
questions.question_html: Feasible region (shaded) for a LPP is shown in the Figure. Minimum of Z = 4x + 3y occurs at the point
questions.explanation: Z=4x+3y 1. (0,8)=24 2.(2,5)=8+15=23 3.(4,3)=16+9=25 4. (9,0)=36+0=36 The correct answer is (2, 5) as it gives the minimum value.

questions.question_text: The function {tex}f(x)=\frac{x^{3}+x^{2}-16 x+20}{x-2}{/tex} is not defined for x = 2. In order to make f(x) continuous at x = 2, f(2) should be defined as
questions.question_html: The function {tex}f(x)=\frac{x^{3}+x^{2}-16 x+20}{x-2}{/tex} is not defined for x = 2. In order to make f(x) continuous at x = 2, f(2) should be defined as
questions.explanation: {tex}f(x)=\frac{x^{3}+x^{2}-16 x+20}{x-2}{/tex} {tex}f(2)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{(x-2)\left(x^{2}+3 x-10\right)}{x-2}{/tex} {tex}f(2)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{(x-2)(x+5)(x-2)}{x-2}{/tex} {tex}f(2)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2}(x+5)(x-2)=0{/tex}

questions.question_text: The number of distinct real roots of {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} {\sin x} & {\cos x} & {\cos x} \\ {\cos x} & {\sin x} & {\cos x} \\ {\cos x} & {\cos x} & {\sin x} \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} in the interval {tex}-\frac{\pi}{4} \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} is
questions.question_html: The number of distinct real roots of {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} {\sin x} & {\cos x} & {\cos x} \\ {\cos x} & {\sin x} & {\cos x} \\ {\cos x} & {\cos x} & {\sin x} \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} in the interval {tex}-\frac{\pi}{4} \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} is
questions.explanation: We have, {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} {\sin x} & {\cos x} & {\cos x} \\ {\cos x} & {\sin x} & {\cos x} \\ {\cos x} & {\cos x} & {\sin x} \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} Applying C 1 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C 1 + C 2 + C 3 , {tex}\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{lll} {2 \cos x+\sin x} & {\cos x} & {\cos x} \\ {2 \cos x+\sin x} & {\sin x} & {\cos x} \\ {2 \cos x+\sin x} & {\cos x} & {\sin x} \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(2 \cos x+\sin x)\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {\cos x} & {\cos x} \\ {1} & {\sin x} & {\cos x} \\ {1} & {\cos x} & {\sin x} \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} [Applying R 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R 2 - R 1 and R 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R 3 - R 1 ] {tex}\Rightarrow (2 \cos x+\sin x)\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {\cos x} & {\cos x} \\ {0} & {\sin x-\cos x} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {\sin x-\cos x} \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad(2 \cos x+\sin x)\left(1-(\sin x-\cos x)^{2}\right]=0{/tex} (expanding along C 1 ) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad(2 \cos x+\sin x)(\sin x-\cos x)^{2}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 cos x = -sin x or sin x = cos x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = -2, which is not possible as for {tex}-\frac{\pi}{4} \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} , we get -1 {tex}\leq{/tex} tan x {tex}\leq{/tex} 1. or tan x = 1 So, only one real root exist.

questions.question_text: Let A be the set of all 50 students of Class X in a school. Let f: A {tex}\to{/tex} N be function defined by f (x) = roll number of the student, f is
questions.question_html: Let A be the set of all 50 students of Class X in a school. Let f: A {tex}\to{/tex} N be function defined by f (x) = roll number of the student, f is
questions.explanation: Since no two different students of the class can have the same roll number. Therefore, f is one-one, also f(A) = Range of f = {1,2,3,4, ...... 50} {tex}\ne{/tex} N i.e. range of f is not same as its co-domain. So,f is not onto.

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 46 to 50: Two farmers Ramakishan and Gurucharan Singh cultivate only three varieties of rice namely Basmati, Permal and Naura. The sale (in rupees) of these varieties of rice by both the farmers in the month of September and October are given by the following matrices A and B. September sales (in Rupees) A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10,000 & 20,000 & 30,000 \\ 50,000 & 30,000 & 10,000 \end{array}\right] \begin{gathered} \text { Ramakishan } \\ \text { Gurucharan } \end{gathered}{/tex} October sales (in Rupees) B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5,000 & 10,000 & 6,000 \\ 20,000 & 10,000 & 10,000 \end{array}\right] \begin{gathered} \text { Ramakishan } \\ \text { Gurucharan } \end{gathered}{/tex} The total sales in September and October for each farmer in each variety can be represented as ________.
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 46 to 50:

Two farmers Ramakishan and Gurucharan Singh cultivate only three varieties of rice namely Basmati, Permal and Naura. The sale (in rupees) of these varieties of rice by both the farmers in the month of September and October are given by the following matrices A and B.

September sales (in Rupees)
A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10,000 & 20,000 & 30,000 \\ 50,000 & 30,000 & 10,000 \end{array}\right] \begin{gathered} \text { Ramakishan } \\ \text { Gurucharan } \end{gathered}{/tex}
October sales (in Rupees)
B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5,000 & 10,000 & 6,000 \\ 20,000 & 10,000 & 10,000 \end{array}\right] \begin{gathered} \text { Ramakishan } \\ \text { Gurucharan } \end{gathered}{/tex}


The total sales in September and October for each farmer in each variety can be represented as ________.
questions.explanation: A + B

questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 46 to 50:

Two farmers Ramakishan and Gurucharan Singh cultivate only three varieties of rice namely Basmati, Permal and Naura. The sale (in rupees) of these varieties of rice by both the farmers in the month of September and October are given by the following matrices A and B.

September sales (in Rupees)
A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10,000 & 20,000 & 30,000 \\ 50,000 & 30,000 & 10,000 \end{array}\right] \begin{gathered} \text { Ramakishan } \\ \text { Gurucharan } \end{gathered}{/tex}

The decrease in sales from September to October is given by ________.
questions.explanation: A - B
